missed_deficiency_types.description: CRLs for combination products (drug-device) reference PMA (Premarket Approval) deficiencies for the device component. The taxonomy has no category for device-specific deficiencies in combination product applications.
CRLs sometimes include postmarketing requirements under 505(o)(3) or 506B as a formal section. These are regulatory commitments that the taxonomy does not track as a distinct deficiency type.

Good Clinical Practice (GCP) inspection findings at clinical trial sites, including unblinding events, protocol deviations, and data quality issues. Distinct from facility GMP inspections and from clinical efficacy assessment.
For biologics, FDA assigns a four-letter suffix for inclusion in the proper name. This is a distinct regulatory step referenced in CRLs for biosimilar and biologic products.

miscategorization_patterns.pattern: Impurity qualification studies labeled as NONCLINICAL instead of CMC/Impurities
miscategorization_patterns.suggested_fix: When a CRL NONCLINICAL section discusses impurity specifications exceeding ICH Q3B(R2) qualification thresholds and requires toxicological qualification studies, the primary category should be CAT-03-G (Impurities) with a secondary tag of CAT-05 (Nonclinical). The CRL section header 'NONCLINICAL' is misleading - the root deficiency is an impurity specification issue requiring qualification data, not a standalone nonclinical safety study gap. Apply a dual-tagging approach: primary=CAT-03-G, secondary=CAT-05-A.

taxonomy_observations: The taxonomy achieves 95.5% categorization accuracy and 97.2% coverage across 179 deficiencies in 30 sampled CRLs, indicating strong overall quality.

CAT-04-A (Safety Update) is confirmed as overwhelmingly boilerplate - present in 29 of 30 CRLs (96.7%) with identical 21 CFR 314.50(d)(5)(vi)(b) language. The 75.4% estimate in the taxonomy may undercount this.

CAT-01 (Labeling) subcategories are well-validated: PI format (CAT-01-A), carton/container (CAT-01-B), proprietary name (CAT-01-C), and medication guide (CAT-01-D) all appeared in the sample with clear CRL text matches.

18 of 40 taxonomy subcategories (45%) were not observed in the 30-CRL sample. Most of these are lower-frequency categories (CAT-06 through CAT-10) that would require a larger sample or targeted selection to validate.

The boundary between NONCLINICAL (CAT-05) and IMPURITIES (CAT-03-G) is the primary source of miscategorization. FDA CRL letters sometimes place impurity qualification discussions under a 'NONCLINICAL' section header, which can mislead taxonomy assignment.
Facility inspection deficiencies (CAT-02-C) are the most common non-labeling, non-safety-update deficiency type, appearing in 14 of 30 CRLs (46.7%).
The taxonomy correctly identifies FACILITY_GMP as a high-frequency category (44.5% per taxonomy vs. 46.7% observed in sample).

Biosimilar-specific deficiencies (comparative analytical assessment, highly similar determination) map reasonably well to existing CMC categories (CAT-03-A, CAT-03-E) but could benefit from a dedicated subcategory.
COVID-19 inspection delays are referenced in several 2020-2021 CRLs under facility inspection sections, representing a temporal phenomenon that the taxonomy does not need to separately capture.

The HUMAN FACTORS category (CAT-07) is well-validated by the Takeda and Luye CRLs, confirming that HF validation study failures and use-related risk analyses are real deficiency types found in CRL text.
